topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sickening thud of a dropped stone. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling so violently I had to grip the mouse with both hands just to keep it steady. *Denied.* The single word burned itself onto my retinas. I reread the rejection letter, the carefully worded bureaucratic language mocking my hopes. A cold, clammy feeling started in my chest, spreading outward until my breath hitched, shallow and ragged. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the legs against the linoleum sounding deafening in the quiet dorm room. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent campus green, feeling suddenly exposed and terribly small. Every shadow seemed to deepen, and a knot tightened painfully beneath my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

I kept refreshing the portal, a desperate, twitching habit. Each new page loaded with the same sterile confirmation, the finality of the decision hammering against my skull. My palms were slick with sweat, and I could feel the rapid drumming of my pulse echoing in my ears. I tried to focus on the textbook open on my desk, but the words swam, dissolving into meaningless shapes. A sharp, dizzying wave washed over me, and I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to physically block out the stark reality displayed on the monitor. When I finally forced my eyes open, the room seemed too bright, too loud. My throat constricted, and I swallowed hard, the dryness making my mouth feel like sandpaper. I wanted to run, to flee the silence, but my feet felt cemented to the floor.

[story 3]

"No." The word echoed in the empty hallway of my apartment. I stood there, rooted to the spot, the crumpled envelope still clutched in my hand. My hands were shaking so badly I could barely hold the paper flat. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, a tightness that made the air feel thin and insufficient. I started pacing, a tight, frantic circuit around the small living room. Each step was clumsy, weighted down by an internal tremor. I kept glancing toward the door, expecting someone to burst in, expecting some authority figure to appear and somehow reverse this impossible outcome. The silence of my own apartment amplified the sound of my own rapid, shallow breathing, a frantic rhythm I couldn't seem to control.
